document_title: A Better Ear Is Not a Recovery

slides.headline:
Now a machine can
hear them through
the ship noise.

Two gates. One
white whale.
slides.body: NOAA Fisheries, Microsoft AI for Good and Alaska Fish and Game built a deep learning listener in two stages. Stage one flags a sound. Stage two names the species behind it.

slides.headline:
Down almost 80
percent.
slides.body: Cook Inlet's belugas are a genetically distinct population, cut off from every other Alaska stock. They fell from about 1,300 whales in 1979 to 279 by 2018, and were listed endangered in 2008.

slides.headline:
A whale in a wall
of noise.
slides.body: Ocean noise is one of the biggest threats NOAA lists for these belugas. In a crowded inlet a single call can vanish under a passing ship. Hearing them at all is the first problem.

slides.headline:
It learns the calls
it used to miss.
slides.body: The model uses contrastive audio language models and active learning. It sorts a sound into beluga, killer whale or humpback, flags the calls it is unsure of for a person to label, and firms up a daily read of where belugas are.

slides.headline:
Ears below.
Eyes above.
slides.body: A separate NOAA led effort, with the U.S. Geological Survey, Microsoft AI for Good and the Naval Research Laboratory, is teaching a cloud model to spot the same whales in satellite images.

slides.headline:
331 today. Still far
below 1979.
annotations: NOAA says 331 may be stabilizing, still about three quarters below the 1,300 of 1979.

slides.headline:
The machine measures
the whale. It cannot
move it.
slides.body: A sharper ear tells managers where and when the belugas are. It does not quiet the ships, refill the prey, or clear the inlet. The hunt has been closed since 2007, and the whales still have not recovered.

slides.headline:
A better ear is
not a recovery.
slides.body: AI can finally hear Alaska's most endangered whale. What would it take to bring it back?

caption:
Cook Inlet has about 331 belugas left, and AI can finally hear them through the ship noise.

NOAA Fisheries, Microsoft AI for Good and Alaska Fish and Game built a two stage listener: one model flags a sound, another names the species. A separate NOAA effort is adding satellite eyes.

These 9 slides walk through the machine, the wall of noise it fights, and one honest chart: 331 today, still about three quarters below the 1,300 counted in 1979.

A sharper ear tells managers where and when the belugas are. It does not quiet the ships or refill the prey. So what would it actually take to turn a clearer signal into more whales?

#CookInletBeluga #MarineBioacoustics #AlaskaScience
first_comment:
Sources for this deck, primary first.

Marine Mammal Science, June 7 2026, https://doi.org/10.1111/mms.70213
NOAA Fisheries, Cook Inlet beluga whale species page, https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/species/beluga-whale
NOAA Fisheries, satellite monitoring of Cook Inlet belugas, https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/alaska/science-data/satellite-monitoring-cook-inlet-beluga-whales

Honesty note. The acoustic paper's methods here are drawn from its published abstract, and no detection accuracy figure is claimed because none appears in that abstract.

Know this story firsthand? Add context or a correction below.
aftercare: Post from a personal profile if possible; the analytical voice travels further from a person than a page. Tue to Thu 8 to 11am AKT are strong slots; cadence is your call.
Verify the PDF uploaded as a document (carousel) post, not flat images, and that all 9 slides swipe in order.
Paste per-slide alt text when you upload so the deck is accessible and ranker-legible.
Confirm the 3 hashtags sit at the very end of the caption and no link is in the body.
Paste the sources block as the first comment within 60 seconds of posting.
Reply to comments in the first 90 minutes; note saves and comments after 48 hours for the ledger.
